coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic metal-organic framework-based molecularly imprinted sensors for selective on-site detection of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Dandan
Zhao, Niao
Wang, Shenghui
Cui, Yahan
Yan, Hongyuan
coredata.dc:description: Designing and constructing simple and selective sensors for the on-site recognition of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) is both significant and challenging. Here, we report the development of molecularly imprinted sensors (Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs) utilizing bimetallic metal-organic frameworks as supporting carriers and catalysts, constructed via surface imprinting for the rapid detection and determination of 2,4-D. Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited oxidase-like activity, catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to yield a blue-colored oxidized TMB in the absence of H2O2. The high affinity of the imprinted layer for 2,4-D selectively reduced the oxidase-like activity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs in the presence of 2,4-D, resulting in a significant decrease in blue color intensity. The absorbance at 650 nm gradually decreased with increasing 2,4-D concentration. Furthermore, we established a colorimetric method based on Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for the rapid detection of 2,4-D in environmental water. This sensing platform exhibited sensitivity and selectivity for detecting 2,4-D, with a low detection limit of 2.26 μM. These findings underscore the outstanding potential of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for environmental chemistry sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted sensors
Bimetallic MOF
Colorimetric detection
Herbicides
2,4-Dichlorophenoxyacetic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytical performance and mechanism of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs
Steady-state kinetic analysis and oxidase-like mechanism of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of (a) the preparation of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs and (b) their application for the highly selective colorimetric detection of 2,4-D.
SEM images of (a-c) Zn/Co-MOF and (d-f) Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs with different sizes. TEM images of (g) Zn/Co-MOF and (h, i) Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs with different sizes.

(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD analysis of Zn/Co-MOF and Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. (c) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption curves and (inset c) pore size distribution of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. XPS analysis of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs: (d) total spectrum, (e) N 1 s. (f) TGA curves of Zn/Co-MOF and Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs.

(a) Adsorption isotherms and (b) fitting curves and of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for 2, 4-D. (c) Adsorption kinetics and (d) fitting curves of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for 2, 4-D. (e) Selectivity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs and Zn/Co-MOF@NIPs. (f) Chemical structures of analytes.

(a) Color changes of different systems. Inset: a) 2, 4-D (5 mg L−1) + TMB; b) 2,4-D (10 mg L−1) + TMB; c) Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs + TMB; d) Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs + 2, 4-D (5 mg L−1) + TMB; e) Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs + 2,4-D (10 mg L−1) + TMB. (b) UV–Vis spectra of the system with 2,4-D at various concentrations. (c) Linear relationship between the ΔA and the level of 2, 4-D. (d) Interference experiments of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic metal-organic framework-based molecularly imprinted sensors for selective on-site detection of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hebei Key Laboratory of Public Health Safety, School of Life Sciences, College of Public Health, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Public Health Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Pharmaceutical Preparations and Excipients, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Pharmaceutical Preparations and Excipients, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Hebei Key Laboratory of Public Health Safety, School of Life Sciences, College of Public Health, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hebei Key Laboratory of Public Health Safety

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple and selective molecularly imprinted sensors (Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs) utilizing bimetallic metal-organic frameworks as supporting carriers and catalysts were constructed via surface imprinting for the rapid detection and determination of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) in environmental water. Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited oxidase-like activity, catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine to yield a blue-colored oxidized TMB in the absence of H2O2. The established colorimetric method based on Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited sensitivity and selectivity for detecting 2,4-D with a low detection limit of 2.26 μM.

Designing and constructing simple and selective sensors for the on-site recognition of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) is both significant and challenging. Here, we report the development of molecularly imprinted sensors (Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs) utilizing bimetallic metal-organic frameworks as supporting carriers and catalysts, constructed via surface imprinting for the rapid detection and determination of 2,4-D. Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited oxidase-like activity, catalyzing the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to yield a blue-colored oxidized TMB in the absence of H2O2. The high affinity of the imprinted layer for 2,4-D selectively reduced the oxidase-like activity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs in the presence of 2,4-D, resulting in a significant decrease in blue color intensity. The absorbance at 650 nm gradually decreased with increasing 2,4-D concentration. Furthermore, we established a colorimetric method based on Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for the rapid detection of 2,4-D in environmental water. This sensing platform exhibited sensitivity and selectivity for detecting 2,4-D, with a low detection limit of 2.26 μM. These findings underscore the outstanding potential of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs for environmental chemistry sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous expansion of agricultural production worldwide, the utilization of pesticides has surged, categorizing existing pesticides into four types based on their biological function: insecticides, rodenticides, herbicides, and fungicides. Among these, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), also recognized as synthetic plant growth hormone, stands out as a principal herbicide targeting dicotyledonous broadleaved weeds. Its application spans across food grain crops, fruit tree plantations, meadows, and lawns. However, excessive and improper application leads to residues in water, soil, and crops, posing significant threats to both the environment and human health. Extensive research has demonstrated the genotoxic, neurotoxic, immunotoxic, reproductive, and carcinogenic risks associated with 2,4-D residues [1]. The World Health Organization's International Agency classified 2,4-D as a Group 2B carcinogen in 2017, affirming its substantial peril to living organisms. Consequently, prioritizing the control and monitoring of 2,4-D presence in the environment is imperative.

Presently, conventional techniques such as liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry have been employed for the quantitative detection of 2,4-D [2–4]. However, these analytical methodologies necessitate intricate pretreatment procedures, expensive equipment, and skilled operators. Thus, there persists a pressing need for the design of a straightforward assay to reliably measure 2,4-D, a challenge within the analytical realm. Colorimetric sensing offers distinct advantages such as portability, simplicity in usage, rapid analysis, and visual readability, garnering considerable attention for on-site screening analyses. Nonetheless, existing colorimetric sensors for 2,4-D detection predominantly rely on organic dye probes [5,6], characterized by low stability, labor-intensive preparation, and preservation requirements. Hence, the development of novel colorimetric sensors tailored for 2,4-D analysis assumes paramount importance.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) represent a promising avenue for designing effective sensing assays. Leveraging tunable pore sizes and exceptional catalytic activity, MOFs play pivotal roles in achieving analytical objectives [7]. The distinctive structures of MOFs cater to the critical requisites for effective catalyst [8–11]. Drawing inspiration from such endeavors, this study fabricates bimetallic MOFs to harness their enhanced catalytic features and outstanding porous structures. Given the specificity required for the prepared sensors, an entity endowed with selective recognition ability is integrated into the fabrication process.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), serving as synthetic recognition materials, mimic natural molecular recognition functions. Offering unique characteristics including structural predictability, recognition specificity, and application versatility, MIPs present exemplary platforms for analytical pursuits such as food inspection, drug monitoring, and environmental assessment [12–14]. Notably, when the imprinted substrate possesses a high specific surface area and excellent catalytic properties, the resultant imprinted sensors can surmount common limitations associated with imprinted materials, such as deep template embedding, low adsorption capacity, and slow mass-transfer rates, while simplifying the analytical workflow and facilitating integration of enrichment and on-site detection. Currently, there are many studies that combine molecular imprinting with spectroscopy [15,16]. However, the construction of MIPs via surface imprinting based on bimetallic MOFs for targeted separation and on-site detection of 2,4-D in environmental water remains unexplored.

In light of the aforementioned discussion, this study employs 2-methylimidazole as the organic ligand and Zn2+ and Co2+ as double cations to construct bimetallic Zn/Co-MOF nanosheets via a one-step hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the sol–gel imprinting process is executed on the Zn/Co-MOF surface using 2,4-D as the template molecule, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) as the functional monomer, and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as the crosslinker for surface imprinting. The resulting Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs are utilized as colorimetric sensors for on-site detection and determination of 2,4-D in complex environmental water, predicated on the inhibitory effect of 2,4-D on the catalytic activity of Zn/Co-MOF nanosheets in the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). While Zn/Co-MOF efficiently catalyzes TMB, resulting in an intense blue color, the presence of 2,4-D impedes TMB oxidation, progressively diminishing the blue signal in a concentration-dependent manner. Furthermore, owing to the modified imprinted layer, the obtained Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibit enhanced sensitivity for 2,4-D detection. The combination of the imprinted layer's high affinity for target molecules and the high surface area and efficient catalytic performance of Zn/Co-MOF renders the fabricated Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs highly selective with exceptional catalytic performance, thereby endowing the established colorimetric sensing platform with robust anti-interference capability, superior recognition efficiency, and remarkable sensing outcomes.
The experiment section is included in Supplementary Material file.

The preparation process of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs and its mechanism for 2,4-D colorimetric sensing are depicted in Fig. 1 . Initially, bimetallic Zn/Co-MOF materials were successfully synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method utilizing a metal doping strategy [17–20]. The resulting Zn/Co-MOF demonstrated the capability to directly catalyze TMB into the blue product oxTMB, showcasing excellent oxidase-like activity. Leveraging this advantage, Zn/Co-MOF was employed as the imprinting substrate, and the imprinting process was executed via a sol–gel route catalyzed by aqueous ammonia on its surface. In this process, APTES served as the functional monomer, TEOS as the cross-linker, and 2,4-D as the template. Upon completion of the reaction, 2,4-D was entirely removed to yield the imprinting material Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs, characterized by high affinity for 2,4-D.

Upon binding of 2,4-D to the specific adsorption sites of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs through specific recognition, the catalytic activity of Zn/Co-MOF in oxidizing TMB is hindered, resulting in decreased oxTMB production and attenuation of the blue signal. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum was employed to monitor the alteration in the blue signal, and based on the correlation between the change in 2,4-D concentration and absorbance, a linear regression equation was established to enable the quantitative detection of 2,4-D.

As illustrated in Fig. 2
(a-c) and Fig. S2
(a-c), the Zn/Co-MOF exhibits a flower-like structure composed of nanosheets with a smooth surface. Following imprinting, Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited a rougher surface with increased thickness compared to Zn/Co-MOF (Fig. 2 (d-f)). Conversely, the spherical agglomeration of Zn/Co-MOF@NIPs is smaller than that of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs ( Fig. S3
(a, b)). Moreover, Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibits an evident core–shell structure, where the light-colored area represents Zn/Co-MOF and the dark-colored area signifies the MIPs imprinting layer [22] (Fig. 2 (g-i)). EDS elemental mapping revealed uniform distribution of C, N, O, Zn, Co, and Si elements in Zn/Co-MOF ( Fig. S4
(a,b)). After the imprinting process, the proportion of Si elements in the EDS spectra of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs ( Fig. S4
(c,d)) significantly increased.

In the FT-IR spectrum of Zn/Co-MOF (Fig. 3
a), the characteristic peak at 1621 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond, while the stretching vibration characteristic peaks of the C-H bond in methyl and imidazole rings appear at 2932 cm−1 and 2820 cm−1 [23]. The emergence of new characteristic absorption peaks near 797 cm−1 and 1062 cm−1 in Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs is attributed to the bending vibration and stretching vibration of the Si-O bond and Si-O-Si bond, respectively [24]. Additionally, the peak at 1557 cm−1 originates from the amino group of APTES, the functional monomer utilized in the imprinting process [25]. The presence of these characteristic absorption peaks confirms the successful introduction of the silane imprinted layer in Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs.

The XRD pattern of Zn/Co-MOF was consistent with previously reported literatures [17,18,26], confirming the successful preparation of Zn/Co-MOF. The main characteristic peaks of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs (Fig. 3 b) are consistent with those of Zn/Co-MOF. However, it is evident that the characteristic peak strength of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs is lower than that of Zn/Co-MOF, attributing to the amorphous structure of the imprinted layer deposited on the Zn/Co-MOF [27]. Additionally, a new diffraction peak of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs appears at 23.22°, indicating the successful formation of amorphous silica [28].

The BET surface area and pore volume of Zn/Co-MOF (85.42 m2 g−1, 0.131 cm3 g−1) were slightly larger than those of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs (29.80 m2 g−1, 0.078 cm3 g−1) ( Fig. S5
and Fig. 3 c), which may facilitate the exposure of abundant effective adsorption sites. During the imprinting process, the imprinting layer formed by Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs covered part of the Zn/Co-MOF binding sites, resulting in the BET surface area and pore volume of Zn/Co-MOF were slightly larger than those of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. The surface chemical properties and elemental composition of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs were analyzed by XPS. Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs mainly consists of Zn, Co, O, N, C, and Si elements, consistent with the EDS results (Fig. 3 d,e and Fig. S6
a-e) [29–34]. The thermal stability of Zn/Co-MOF and Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs was assessed by TGA curves (Fig. 3 f and Fig. S7 ), confirming their excellent thermal stability [35–37].

It is evident that the adsorption capacity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs increased sharply with the rising concentrations of 2,4-D within the tested range, suggesting a high saturation adsorption capacity for 2,4-D (Fig. 4
a). In contrast, Zn/Co-MOF@NIPs exhibited lower adsorption capacity than Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs at equivalent initial concentrations, which was attributed to the denser surface and no discernible molecular transfer channel of Zn/Co-MOF@NIPs [38]. Fig. 4 b illustrates the fitting curves for Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs, revealing larger correlation coefficients in the Langmuir model ( Table S2 ), suggesting that the active sites on the adsorbent surface are uniformly distributed and adsorb single molecular layers [39].

Primarily due to the abundance and availability of adsorption cavities in the imprinted material (Fig. 4 c), Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs rapidly adsorbed 2,4-D, reaching saturation within 10 min. As shown in Fig. 4 d and Table S3 , the pseudo-second-order kinetic model exhibited higher correlation coefficients, indicating that chemisorption dominants the adsorption process [40,41].

The selectivity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs as sorbents towards 2,4-D was evaluated as shown in Fig. 4 e, and Fig. 4 f illustrates the chemical structures of the analytes). Leveraging the imprinted sites tailored for 2,4-D, Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs exhibited the highest adsorption capacity for 2,4-D, while displaying comparatively lower adsorption capacities for other substances. This remarkable disparity in adsorption capacities underscores the outstanding adsorption selectivity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytical performance and mechanism of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs

ce:section.ce:para:
The oxidase-like activity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs was investigated using TMB chromogenic assay. In the presence of this nanozyme, colorless TMB undergoes oxidation by oxygen to form oxTMB with a blue hue, exhibiting a distinct absorption peak at 650 nm [42,43]. Additionally, the peroxidase-like activity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs was evaluated by catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. Based on the corresponding analysis in Supplementary Material from Fig. S8
a and for subsequent convenience, the oxidase-like activity of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs was primarily utilized in the following experiments.

Influencing factors, such as solution pH and reaction temperature, were optimized. As depicted in Fig. S8
(b,c), pH 3.0 and 60 ℃ were determined as the optimal pH and temperature for the reaction system, respectively. It has been reported that artificially synthesized nanozymes demonstrate excellent catalytic stability compared to natural nanozymes. The storage stability of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs was investigated. The results indicated that the oxidase-like catalytic effect of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs remained stable, maintaining over 94 % activity for more than 20 days ( Fig. S8
d), thereby highlighting its exceptional storage stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic analysis and oxidase-like mechanism of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized reaction conditions, the hyperbolic fit parameters Km and Vmax for Zn/Co-MOF and Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs were calculated using TMB as substrates ( Fig. S9 ). A low Km value generally indicates a strong affinity between the catalyst and substrate. From Table S4 , the Km of Zn/Co-MOF was calculated to be 0.679 mM, lower than that of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. This may be attributed to the high specific surface area and porous structure, providing abundant accessible active sites for TMB substrate activation in the oxidation process.

The oxidase-mimicking mechanism of the catalyst is commonly believed to involve nanomaterials catalyzing and oxidizing dissolved oxygen in the reaction system to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) free radicals, which further oxidize the substrate [45]. As shown in Fig. S10
a, compared with the solution under air conditions, the A650 significantly decreased under N2-purged conditions, illustrating the vital role of dissolved O2 in the oxidation process.

Studies [19,20] have indicated that oxygen is catalyzed to different types of ROS in the oxidase-related catalytic process. To further reveal the possible ROS participating in the Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs-mediated catalytic system, selective scavengers, including catalase (CAT), isopropyl alcohol (IP), hydroxylamine, tryptophan (Trp), and anhydrous ethanol, were employed to probe hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide anions (O2·−), singlet oxygen (1O2), and holes created by light (h+), respectively [44,45]. It can be inferred that ·OH radicals and h+ are the major contributors to the catalytic process ( Fig. S10
b-10f, and
Fig. S11 ).

The Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs, bearing 2,4-D-matched recognition sites, selectively capture 2,4-D, thereby occupying the imprinted cavities and consequently reducing the catalytic performance of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. Additionally, fewer oxTMB molecules are produced in the catalytic oxidation of TMB by Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs. Based on this mechanism, we established a colorimetric platform to determine 2,4-D using the Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs-TMB system. Notably, 2,4-D itself cannot catalyze the oxidation of TMB to oxTMB. Fig. 5
a clearly illustrates that the color of the [Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs + TMB] solution becomes lighter with increasing addition of 2,4-D, accompanied by a corresponding decrease in absorbance at 650 nm (Fig. 5 b). ΔA=(A0-Ai), where A0 represents the absorbance of the system without 2,4-D, and Ai denotes the absorbance value of the system with varying concentrations of 2,4-D. The calibration equation relating absorbance to the concentration of 2,4-D is: ΔA=0.06041c-0.04394 (R2 = 0.997). A good linear relationship is observed between ΔA and the concentration of 2,4-D within the range of 6.0–45.24 μM (Fig. 5 c). The detection limit is calculated to be 2.26 μM (signal/noise ratio of 3:1).

To evaluate the anti-interference feature of the established colorimetric platform, potential interfering substances that could coexist in the environment, including metal cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Al3+) and amino acids (Phe, Lys, Thr, Val, Ser, Ala, and Leu), were tested. As shown in Fig. 5 d, a significant change in absorbance intensity is observed in the presence of 2,4-D, while negligible response is observed in these potential interfering substances. These results indicate that the established platform based on the prepared Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs nanomaterial exhibits acceptable selectivity for 2,4-D. This excellent anti-interference performance can primarily be attributed to the specific recognition of the molecularly imprinted nanozyme Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs to 2,4-D, which possesses binding sites and space cavities exactly matching the size and shape of the target, thereby blocking the channels for nanozyme catalysis.

To demonstrate the practicality of the platform, we determined the contents of 2,4-D in environmental water samples from Fuhe River and Qingshui River in Baoding. No 2,4-D was detected. Furthermore, spiked experiments with three concentrations were performed to indicate the feasibility of the platform. The recovery of 2,4-D ranged from 96.5 % to 101.5 % with RSD<4.1 %, indicating that the colorimetric method is a reliable approach for detecting 2,4-D in environmental water, the experimental data are listed in Table S5. Compared to other 2,4-D detection methods [46–55] in Table S6, our method offers comparable performances in terms of detection limit and sensing range.

In this study, bimetallic metal–organic framework-based molecularly imprinted sensors (Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs) were synthesized via a solvothermal method followed by a sol–gel imprinting process for the on-site detection of 2,4-D. The prepared bimetallic Zn/Co-MOF nanosheets exhibited inherent oxidase-like activity, catalyzing the oxidation of TMB to produce the typical color reaction even in the absence of H2O2. By incorporating the imprinted layer onto the Zn/Co-MOF support, the resulting Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs combined excellent oxidase-like performance with recognition specificity for 2,4-D. When 2,4-D was absorbed into the imprinted sites of Zn/Co-MOF@MIPs, their catalytic capability decreased, leading to diminished color information. Exploiting this phenomenon, we established a simple and rapid colorimetric method for detecting 2,4-D, which exhibited an excellent linear range from 6 to 45 μM and a low detection limit of 2.26 μM. We then applied this platform to detect 2,4-D in real water samples, achieving good recovery rates ranging from 96.5 % to 101.5 %. The integration of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with synthesized nano-enzymes for colorimetric detection holds promise for enhancing specificity and convenience in future analytical fields.

Dandan Han: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation. Niao Zhao: Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Shenghui Wang: Validation, Methodology. Yahan Cui: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hongyuan Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82073605, 82373634), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2023201083) the Interdisciplinary Project of Hebei University (DXK202014), Innovation Team Program of Hebei University (IT2023B11, IT2023A06), the Basic Research Program for Universities in Hebei (241791377A), the Medical Science Foundation of Hebei University (2023A01), and the Advanced Talents Incubation Program of Hebei University (521100223203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111318.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D) biomonitoring and epidemiology
Crit. Rev. Toxicol.

Methods. 14 (2020) 561-567. 10.1007/s12161-020-01903-3.

Foods.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic-liquid-modified magnetite nanoparticles for MSPE-GC-MS determination of 2,4-D butyl ester and its metabolites in water, soil, and bottom sediments
